Row1.Rationale: Binimetinib is a selective ATP‑uncompetitive MEK1/2 inhibitor that blocks ERK activation downstream of oncogenic BRAF; encorafenib + binimetinib is now an approved BRAF/MEK doublet in BRAF V600E–mutant NSCLC, where MAPK signaling is the central proliferative driver.[9],[10],[7]

Row2.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma, tumors remain addicted to MAPK signaling; vertical cotargeting of KRAS G12C and MEK suppresses pathway reactivation and enhances cell killing, but MEK (and KRAS G12C) inhibition rapidly induces rebound ERK phosphorylation via multiple RTKs, limiting durability of MEK‑pathway blockade.[1],[4],[5]

Row3.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant lung models, high FGFR1 or EMT‑associated FGFR1 upregulation correlates with reduced sensitivity to MAPK‑pathway inhibitors; KRAS G12C inhibition or MEK inhibition can select FGFR1‑high, EMT‑like clones in which FGFR1 signaling re‑activates ERK and S6, conferring multidrug resistance unless FGFR1 and MAPK are co‑inhibited.[1],[5]

Row4.Rationale: KRAS G12C inhibitor treatment in LUAD induces adaptive upregulation and activation of several RTKs (ERBB2, ERBB3, FGFR1), which converge on RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT to restore downstream signaling; similar multi‑RTK rebound is a known mechanism of MEK‑inhibitor resistance, implying that binimetinib monotherapy in KRAS‑mutant NSCLC will be bypassed by RTK‑driven MAPK reactivation.[1],[4],[5]

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: PI3K–AKT signaling downstream of RTK–WT RAS
Row5.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma, PI3K/AKT activation is largely RTK‑dependent and complements mutant KRAS‑driven RAF/MEK/ERK; MEK inhibition or KRAS G12C inhibition triggers broad RTK–WT RAS signaling that restores PI3K/AKT, allowing survival despite ERK blockade, so persistent or rebound PI3K–AKT undermines MEK‑targeted strategies.[1],[4],[5]

Row6.Rationale: The MAPK cascade is tightly interconnected with PI3K/AKT; inhibition of one arm (e.g., MEK) often produces compensatory activation of the other through feedback and RTK crosstalk, a general mechanism of resistance to MAPK inhibitors in lung and other cancers, indicating that binimetinib will be more effective in combinations that also constrain PI3K/AKT or upstream RTKs.[5],[8]

Row7.Rationale: MEK or KRAS G12C inhibition in RAS‑mutant tumors causes rebound activation of multiple RTKs that signal via SHP2/SOS1 to WT RAS, driving renewed RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT activation; SHP2 or SOS1 inhibition enhances MEK‑inhibitor efficacy in preclinical RAS‑mutant lung models, supporting SHP2/SOS1 as critical resistance nodes for binimetinib‑based regimens.[1],[4]

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: EMT program linked to RTK/MAPK re‑wiring
Row8.Rationale: KRAS‑mutant LUAD cells exposed to KRAS G12C inhibition acquire EMT features with ZEB1‑driven FGFR1 upregulation; EMT is associated with MAPK‑pathway inhibitor resistance in lung cancer and promotes a shift from ERBB‑ to FGFR‑dominated feedback, enabling MAPK reactivation and escape from MEK blockade.[1],[5]

Row9.Rationale: HSP90 chaperones many kinases in RTK/RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT cascades; in KRAS G12C‑mutant LUAD, HSP90 inhibition combined with KRAS G12C blockade overcomes multidrug resistance and induces tumor regression, implying that HSP90‑dependent stabilization of MAPK and parallel pathways can sustain signaling despite MEK inhibition and that HSP90 co‑targeting could potentiate binimetinib.[1],[8]

Row10.Rationale: BRAF V600E activates RAF/MEK/ERK in ~1–5% of lung adenocarcinomas; dual BRAF/MEK inhibition (dabrafenib + trametinib, encorafenib + binimetinib) achieves high response rates and durable control, showing that direct suppression of BRAF‑driven MAPK with a MEK inhibitor is a key sensitizing mechanism in this molecular subset.[6],[7],[9]

Row11.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, loss or downregulation of the phosphatase PHLPP blunts EGFR‑TKI–mediated suppression of both AKT and ERK, leading to reduced apoptosis and clinical resistance; because MEK inhibitors act within the same ERK arm, low PHLPP creates a context where dual AKT/ERK activation can diminish the impact of MEK blockade by binimetinib.[17]

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: RTK bypass (MET, HER2, others) into MAPK
Row12.Rationale: Clinical and translational NSCLC data show that resistance to targeted therapies frequently arises via bypass RTK activation (e.g., MET, HER2, AXL), which re‑engages PI3K/AKT and MEK/ERK despite upstream inhibition; such bypass can similarly restore downstream signaling under MEK inhibition, indicating that RTK‑bypass MAPK reactivation is a generic resistance route for binimetinib‑containing strategies.[6],[8],[9]
